options: A genital tract infection resulting from bacterial invasion during or after labor or abortion
A urinary tract infection after delivery
s3.explain: Puerperal sepsis is specifically a genital tract infection from bacterial invasion during or after labor or abortion.

s3.q: The timing window for puerperal sepsis includes:
options: During labor, after labor, or after abortion
s3.explain: The timing window is broad: during labor, after labor, or after abortion.

s4.q: The single most common causative organism in puerperal sepsis is:
s4.explain: Anaerobic streptococci is the single most common organism. Remember the mnemonic A-G-A: Aerobic Gram+/Gram−, Anaerobic Gram+ (commonest) + Gram−.

s4.q: Which of the following is an anaerobic Gram-negative organism causing puerperal sepsis?
s4.explain: Clostridium welchii and Bacteroids are anaerobic Gram-negative organisms. Hemolytic streptococcus A and Staph aureus are aerobic Gram-positive.

s5.q: The endogenous source of puerperal sepsis is:
options: Anaerobic streptococci normally present in the genital tract that become pathogenic in devitalized tissue
s5.explain: Endogenous: organisms normally present (e.g., anaerobic streptococci) become pathogenic in presence of devitalized tissues.

s5.q: Which mode of infection is considered more insidious?
s5.explain: The endogenous pathway is more insidious — harmless commensals before delivery become virulent once devitalized tissue provides a substrate.

s6.q: Which of the following is a local predisposing factor for puerperal sepsis?
s6.explain: Prolonged labor is a local risk factor. Anemia, diabetes, and debilitating diseases are general predisposing factors.

s6.q: The mnemonic for local predisposing factors (7) is P-P-I-L-M-R-I. The 'M' stands for:
s6.explain: P-P-I-L-M-R-I: PROM, Prolonged labor, Instrumental delivery, Lacerations, Marked blood loss, Retained products, Improper aseptic technique.

s7.q: The primary sites of puerperal infection include:
options: Lacerations of the vulva/vagina/cervix and the placental site
s7.explain: Primary sites: (1) lacerations of vulva/vagina/cervix, (2) uterus — placental site or retained products.

s7.q: Secondary spread from primary puerperal infection can occur via:
options: Pelvic spread (PID, parametritis, abscess) and blood spread (bacteremia/septicemia)
s7.explain: Pelvic spread → acute PID, parametritis, pelvic abscess. Blood spread → bacteremia or septicemia.

s8.q: The classic presentation of puerperal sepsis occurs:
s8.explain: Symptoms appear shortly (48-72 hours) following labor or abortion: fever/rigors, abdominal pain, offensive vaginal discharge.

s8.q: The diagnostic temperature criterion for puerperal sepsis is:
s8.explain: Temperature >38°C for 24 hours is the key diagnostic criterion on general examination.

s9.q: An early complication of puerperal sepsis is:
options: Spread to secondary sites e.g. pelvic abscess
s9.explain: Early: spread to secondary sites and complications of septicemia/pyemia. Remote: chronic PID, infertility, dyspareunia.

s9.q: Which of the following is a remote complication of puerperal sepsis?
options: Chronic pelvic inflammatory disease and infertility
s9.explain: Remote complications include chronic PID, infertility, dyspareunia, menorrhagia, and intestinal obstruction. Pelvic abscess and septicemia are early.

s10.q: Essential investigations in suspected puerperal sepsis include all EXCEPT:
options: Swab from cervix and upper vagina for aerobic and anaerobic culture
CT scan of the abdomen routinely
s10.explain: Investigations: blood picture, urine analysis/culture, cervical swab (aerobic + anaerobic), blood culture, U/S for retained parts/abscess. CT is not routine.

s10.q: Why must cervical swabs cover both aerobic and anaerobic cultures?
options: Most cases are caused by anaerobic streptococci which a standard aerobic-only culture would miss
s10.explain: Anaerobic streptococci are the most common organism. An aerobic-only culture would miss them, leading to incorrect treatment.

s11.q: Which of the following is an intranatal preventive measure for puerperal sepsis?
options: Prophylactic antibiotics in PROM and prolonged/instrumental delivery
s11.explain: Intranatal measures: strict asepsis, minimize vaginal exams, avoid excessive blood loss, suture lacerations immediately, prophylactic antibiotics for PROM/prolonged labor.

s11.q: The prevention triad for puerperal sepsis follows the order:
s11.explain: Chronological triad: Antenatal (diet, treat anemia/infection) → Intranatal (asepsis, antibiotics) → Postnatal (perineal care, isolation).

s12.q: The recommended empirical antibiotic regimen for puerperal sepsis includes:
s12.explain: Triple therapy: broad-spectrum (ampicillin/cephalosporin) + gentamycin + metronidazole. Or clindamycin + gentamycin. Metronidazole covers anaerobes.

s12.q: Which of the following is a general measure in treating puerperal sepsis?
options: Isolation in a separate room or fever hospital
s12.explain: General measures: isolation, light nutritious diet with plenty of fluids, IV fluids, correction of anemia.

s12.q: Antitoxin serum is specifically indicated in puerperal sepsis caused by:
s12.explain: Antitoxin serum is given specifically for Cl. welchii infection.
